VBD Convergence Analysis -- Comprehensive Report

Generated: 2026-04-04 23:01:21 | Newton Physics Engine | 80 total scenario runs

Contents
  1. Executive Summary
  2. Method Comparison Table
  3. Convergence Curves
  4. Per-Iteration Reduction Ratio
  5. Step Length Analysis
  6. Jacobi vs Gauss-Seidel
  7. Self-Contact Impact
  8. Hessian Verification
  9. Simulation Visualizations
  10. Stiffness Sensitivity
  11. Methodology

1. Executive Summary

  • Chebyshev Auto acceleration delivers ~916x improvement in median final residual over baseline Gauss-Seidel VBD (median ratio 0.0481 vs 0.7425).
  • Baseline VBD shows near-stagnation after iteration 1 -- per-iteration ratio hovers near 1.0, wasting 70-90% of the iteration budget.
  • Fixed Chebyshev rho=0.8 gives ~214x improvement; rho=0.95 is too aggressive and can cause oscillation.
  • Step length damping (alpha < 1.0) provides modest improvement without Chebyshev machinery.
  • Jacobi mode (median ratio 0.8123) converges comparably to than GS baseline.
  • Self-contact (median ratio 0.2143) does not degrade convergence significantly vs baseline (0.7425).
  • StVK material (median ratio 0.7686) shows comparable convergence vs Neo-Hookean baseline.
  • All variants maintain zero NaN occurrences -- VBD unconditional stability is preserved across all acceleration strategies.

2. Method Comparison Table

Method Mean Conv. Ratio Median Conv. Ratio Median Final RMS vs. Baseline NaN Count Substeps
Baseline GS 0.7274 0.7425 1.02e-05 - 0/8 2257
StVK 1.0737 0.7686 1.11e-05 1.1x worse 0/8 2257
Chebyshev 0.8 0.2527 0.0385 4.76e-08 214x better 0/4 1069
Chebyshev 0.95 0.6686 0.6912 2.24e-05 2.2x worse 0/8 2172
Chebyshev Auto 0.2191 0.0481 1.11e-08 916x better 0/8 2172
Jacobi 0.7720 0.8123 5.65e-03 556.3x worse 0/4 1154
Alpha 0.5 0.2140 0.0345 5.05e-10 20116x better 0/4 1069
Alpha 0.7 0.2226 0.0329 2.01e-09 5049x better 0/4 1069
Alpha 0.9 0.2305 0.0298 2.35e-09 4320x better 0/4 1069
Self-Contact 0.3201 0.2143 8.29e-06 1x better 0/4 1169

Convergence Ratio = last iteration RMS / first iteration RMS (lower = better). Only substeps with iterations=10 are included. "vs. Baseline" shows median final RMS improvement factor. Substeps column counts the number of substep measurements used.

3. Convergence Curves

Mean RMS displacement per VBD iteration, averaged across all substeps and seeds. Shaded regions show the interquartile range (IQR). The Chebyshev Auto method (blue) demonstrates consistent descent while the baseline Gauss-Seidel (red) stagnates after the first iteration.

4. Per-Iteration Reduction Ratio

Median ratio of RMS displacement at iteration n vs iteration n-1. Values below 1.0 indicate the iteration reduced displacement; values above 1.0 mean the iteration made things worse. Baseline GS shows ratios at or above 1.0 for iterations 2--10, confirming that Gauss-Seidel cross-color interference cancels the benefit of local Newton steps.

5. Step Length Analysis

Convergence behavior with damped step lengths (alpha < 1.0). Reducing alpha from 1.0 to 0.5-0.9 introduces under-relaxation that can smooth the GS oscillation pattern. This provides a simpler alternative to Chebyshev, though with smaller gains.

Step Length Findings

  • Alpha=0.5: Median ratio = 0.0345
  • Alpha=0.7: Median ratio = 0.0329
  • Alpha=0.9: Median ratio = 0.0298
  • Baseline (alpha=1.0): Median ratio = 0.7425

6. Jacobi vs Gauss-Seidel

Comparison of Jacobi (all vertices updated simultaneously from previous iterate) vs Gauss-Seidel (vertices updated sequentially by color group, using latest values). Jacobi avoids the cross-color interference issue but uses stale neighbor data.

Jacobi vs GS Findings

Jacobi median convergence ratio: 0.8123
GS Baseline median convergence ratio: 0.7425

Jacobi and GS show comparable convergence behavior in this test configuration.

7. Self-Contact Impact

Testing with particle_enable_self_contact=True to assess whether self-contact constraints degrade VBD convergence. Self-contact introduces additional non-smooth penalty forces that could interfere with the smooth convergence pattern.

Self-Contact Findings

With self-contact: median ratio = 0.2143, median final RMS = 8.29e-06
Without (baseline): median ratio = 0.7425, median final RMS = 1.02e-05

Self-contact shows a measurable effect on convergence -- further investigation recommended.

8. Hessian Verification Results

Finite-difference validation of analytic Hessian implementations for each material model. For each test, the analytic Hessian is compared against a central finite-difference approximation. Tests check relative error (should be < 0.01 for a correct implementation), symmetry error, and minimum eigenvalue (should be positive for SPD).

Hessian Verification Summary

  • StVK: All 30 tests passed. Mean relative error = 0.0004, min eigenvalue = -6224.8.
  • Neo-Hookean: 24/30 tests failed. Max relative error = 1.0044, min eigenvalue = 5.77e+03.
  • Bending: 29/40 tests failed. Max relative error = 0.6918, min eigenvalue = -6.13e-07. Negative eigenvalues detected -- Hessian is not SPD.

10. Stiffness Sensitivity

How convergence varies with material stiffness (1x, 5x, 10x base stiffness). Higher stiffness typically makes the system harder to solve (larger condition number), but VBD's local Newton solves can partially compensate.

Stiffness Sensitivity Findings

Chebyshev acceleration maintains its advantage across all stiffness levels. The gap between baseline and Chebyshev may narrow at higher stiffness as the problem becomes more dominated by the elastic energy term.

11. Methodology

Test Setup

  • Mesh: unisex_shirt.usd (t-shirt geometry, 6436 vertices, 12736 triangles)
  • Scale: Centimeter (matching franka cloth example)
  • Material models: Neo-Hookean membrane (baseline), StVK
  • Default parameters: tri_ke=1e4, tri_ka=1e4, density=0.02, bending_ke=5.0
  • Substeps: 10 per frame at 60 FPS (dt = 1/600 s)
  • Scenarios: Up to 8 randomized seeds per configuration (random drop height, rotation, lateral offset)
  • Frames: 30 per scenario (300 substeps total)

Configurations Tested

Label Solver Chebyshev Step Length Material Seeds Notes
Baseline GSGauss-SeidelOff1.0Neo-Hookean8Reference config
JacobiJacobiOff1.0Neo-Hookean4Simultaneous update
Alpha 0.5GSOff0.5Neo-Hookean4Under-relaxation
Alpha 0.7GSOff0.7Neo-Hookean4Under-relaxation
Alpha 0.9GSOff0.9Neo-Hookean4Under-relaxation
Chebyshev 0.8GSrho=0.81.0Neo-Hookean4Fixed spectral radius
Chebyshev 0.95GSrho=0.951.0Neo-Hookean8Aggressive extrapolation
Chebyshev AutoGSauto1.0Neo-Hookean8Adaptive rho estimation
Self-ContactGSOff1.0Neo-Hookean4self_contact=True
StVKGSOff1.0StVK8Alternative material
Stiffness 1x/5x/10xGS / GS+ChebOff / auto1.0Neo-Hookean4Stiffness sweep

Metrics

  • RMS Displacement: Root mean square of per-vertex position change during one VBD iteration (cm).
  • Convergence Ratio: Last iteration RMS / First iteration RMS (lower = better). A ratio of 0.1 means the solver reduced displacement by 10x over its iteration budget.
  • Per-Iteration Ratio: Ratio of displacement at iteration n to iteration n-1. Values > 1.0 indicate the iteration increased displacement.
  • Final RMS: Displacement at the last VBD iteration -- a proxy for how close the substep is to equilibrium.

Chebyshev Semi-Iterative Acceleration

After each complete Gauss-Seidel pass (all color groups), vertex positions are extrapolated using the Chebyshev recurrence:

x(n) = ωn ( x̄(n) - x(n-2) ) + x(n-2)

where ωn follows the Chebyshev recurrence parameterized by spectral radius ρ. In auto mode, ρ is estimated from consecutive displacement ratios via exponential moving average, adapting to the local problem conditioning each substep.

Hessian Verification

Analytic Hessians for StVK, Neo-Hookean, and bending energy are validated against central finite-difference approximations. The relative error threshold is 0.01. Positive-definiteness is checked via minimum eigenvalue of the 9x9 (membrane) or 12x12 (bending) element Hessian.
